anti_patterns: Using Opus for a specialist that follows a checklist
Using Sonnet for Leo when reasoning about cross-domain trade-offs
Running AI for deterministic tasks that scripts can handle at zero cost
how_to_apply: Leo: Opus (or Mythos when available) — highest reasoning depth for orchestration, cross-domain decisions, and owner communication
Specialists: Sonnet always — they are ephemeral sub-agents that execute playbooks, they don't decide. Sonnet is more than capable for execution tasks
Scripts (validate, build-index, check-stale) cost zero tokens — this is the Token Economy principle at its purest: deterministic work never touches an LLM
Scales with future model releases: Mythos → Opus → Sonnet → future lighter models. The principle stays, the model names change
content.responsibility: Leo enforces model selection when delegating to specialists. Owner sets the orchestration model. Scripts are the responsibility of the core maintainer.
content.rule: Model hierarchy by role: Leo = Opus (orchestration and judgment), Specialists = Sonnet (execution). Most expensive model for thinking, cheapest for execution. Scripts handle all deterministic work at zero token cost.
content.why: AI should spend tokens on thinking and judgment, not on execution. Specialists execute — they follow playbooks, they don't make strategic decisions. Leo reasons about what to do, who to delegate to, what the trade-offs are. The cost of using Opus for a specialist that follows a checklist is waste. The cost of using Sonnet for Leo when he needs to reason about cross-domain trade-offs is a false economy.
